description: A step-by-step dialogue example showing the Self-Ask prompting method, where the LLM decomposes a complex question into follow-up sub-questions, issues external search queries for each, retrieves answers, and synthesizes a final answer.
teaching_value: Concretely illustrates how Self-Ask breaks multi-hop reasoning into explicit intermediate steps with external search, helping students understand how LLMs can be guided to decompose problems rather than answer in one shot.
when_to_show: Show when introducing retrieval-augmented or decomposition-based prompting strategies, especially to contrast with standard single-step prompting.
suggested_caption: Self-Ask: LLM decomposes questions and queries external search. (Press et al. 2022)

description: A diagram comparing four reasoning strategies side by side: Input→Output (standard), Chain-of-Thought, Self-Consistency with CoT, and Tree of Thoughts — showing how ToT explores a branching tree of intermediate reasoning steps rather than a single linear chain.
teaching_value: Visually contrasts the progression from simple prompting to tree-structured exploration, helping students understand why ToT can find better solutions by evaluating multiple reasoning paths rather than committing to one.
when_to_show: Show when teaching advanced reasoning prompting methods, particularly to illustrate the conceptual leap from linear CoT to tree-based deliberate search.
suggested_caption: Tree of Thoughts explores branching reasoning paths vs. linear CoT. (Yao et al. 2022)

description: A side-by-side comparison of Chain-of-Thought (CoT) and Program of Thoughts (PoT) prompting on a math word problem, showing CoT producing natural language reasoning steps while PoT generates Python code that is executed by an interpreter to compute the answer.
teaching_value: Makes the key distinction between CoT (reasoning in natural language, prone to arithmetic errors) and PoT (offloading computation to code execution) concrete and immediately understandable through a worked example.
when_to_show: Show when explaining why offloading computation to a Python interpreter improves reliability over pure natural language reasoning chains.
suggested_caption: PoT generates executable code; CoT reasons in natural language. (Chen et al. 2022)

description: A formatted text example showing the TALM API call structure, with special tokens |tool-call, tool input, |result, and |output demarcating where the LM generates a tool request, where the external API result is inserted, and where the final answer is produced.
teaching_value: Makes the abstract concept of tool-augmented LMs concrete by showing the exact token-level format used to interleave LM generation with external API calls, helping students understand the mechanics of tool use.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how LLMs are structured to call external tools, especially before discussing Toolformer to establish the general pattern.
suggested_caption: TALM uses special tokens to structure tool calls and results. (Parisi et al. 2022)

description: A line graph showing model performance (e.g., accuracy or F1) across multiple self-play iterations of TALM, demonstrating that iterative bootstrapping of tool-use examples progressively improves the model's performance on downstream tasks.
teaching_value: Provides empirical evidence that the self-play bootstrapping loop in TALM is effective, helping students understand why iterative dataset expansion with tool feedback leads to measurable gains.
when_to_show: Show when discussing the training methodology of TALM to justify why the self-play loop is used rather than a fixed dataset.
suggested_caption: TALM self-play iterations progressively boost task performance. (Parisi et al. 2022)

description: A pipeline diagram illustrating how Toolformer is built: starting from raw text, generating candidate API call annotations via few-shot prompting, executing the API calls, filtering annotations by whether they reduce loss on future tokens, and fine-tuning the LM on the filtered dataset.
teaching_value: Shows the full self-supervised training pipeline for Toolformer, helping students understand how a model can learn to use tools without large-scale human annotation by using its own predictions as a training signal.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how Toolformer is trained, particularly to illustrate the self-supervised annotation and filtering steps that make it data-efficient.
suggested_caption: Toolformer pipeline: annotate, execute, filter, and fine-tune. (Schick et al. 2023)

description: A text example showing how Toolformer annotates a sentence with API calls inline — e.g., inserting a calculator or search API call with arguments and results directly into the text, illustrating the annotation format used to create training data.
teaching_value: Concretely shows students what the annotated training data looks like for Toolformer, making it clear how API calls are embedded in natural text and how results are incorporated, grounding the abstract pipeline in a real example.
when_to_show: Show alongside the Toolformer pipeline diagram to give a concrete data-level example of what the annotation step produces.
suggested_caption: Toolformer annotates text with inline API calls and results. (Schick et al. 2023)

source_author: Blanchard, Pierre, Higham, Desmond J, Higham, Nicholas J
alt: Scatter plots of errors and error bounds, scaled by unit roundoff, over $2025$ vectors in ${\mathbb{R}}^{10}$ for log-sum-exp algorithms in fp16. See the text for a description of the axes. Upper left: basic implementation of log-sum-exp from Algorithm 3.1. According to the error analysis all points should lie below the reference line $y = x$ (shown in red). Upper right: corresponding results for the shifted implementation of log-sum-exp in Algorithm 4.1. Lower: scaled error from Algorithm 3.1 versus scaled error from Algorithm 4.1.
description: Three scatter plots for fp16 log-sum-exp experiments over many 10D input vectors: (top-left) scaled forward error vs scaled error bound for the basic log-sum-exp (Algorithm 3.1) with a red y=x reference line; (top-right) the same for the shifted log-sum-exp (Algorithm 4.1) using its theoretical bound; (bottom) scaled error of Algorithm 3.1 (x-axis) versus scaled error of Algorithm 4.1 (y-axis), comparing the two implementations point-by-point. Cases where the basic algorithm overflowed are omitted.
teaching_value: Helps students connect floating-point error analysis to empirical behavior: whether computed errors respect proven bounds, how shifting avoids overflow in fp16, and how two mathematically equivalent formulations can yield different numerical errors.
when_to_show: Show after introducing basic vs shifted log-sum-exp formulas and discussing fp16 overflow thresholds; use it to prompt learners to interpret scatter plots (bound satisfaction, outliers) and to justify why shifting is used in practice.
suggested_caption: fp16 log-sum-exp errors and bounds: basic vs shifted (Blanchard et al., 2021).

source_author: Blanchard, Pierre, Higham, Desmond J, Higham, Nicholas J
alt: Scatter plots of errors, scaled by unit roundoff, for softmax algorithms in fp16. See the text for a description of the axes. Reference line is $y = x$.
description: Scatter plots of fp16 softmax errors (scaled by unit roundoff) for different softmax algorithms, with a red y=x reference line, illustrating how observed errors compare across implementations and against a baseline relationship.
teaching_value: Supports learning how softmax numerical stability depends on implementation details in low precision, and how to read scaled-error scatter plots to compare algorithms’ accuracy and variability.
when_to_show: Show when transitioning from log-sum-exp to softmax, especially after explaining that softmax uses exponentials and normalization; use it to have students identify which algorithm clusters closer to the y=x line and discuss why.
suggested_caption: Scaled fp16 softmax errors across algorithms (Blanchard et al., 2021).

source_author: Blanchard, Pierre, Higham, Desmond J, Higham, Nicholas J
alt: Sum of entries of computed softmax vector for Algorithm 3.1 (red circles), analyzed in Theorem 3.3, and the alternative (blue crosses) analyzed in Theorem 3.4.
description: Plot of the sum of entries of computed softmax vectors for Algorithm 3.1 (red circles) and an alternative softmax algorithm (blue crosses), across many test cases; the x-axis is an arbitrary ordering of test vectors and the y-values show deviation of the softmax sum from the ideal value 1.
teaching_value: Teaches a simple, interpretable diagnostic for softmax accuracy: whether probabilities sum to 1, and how alternative formulations can degrade normalization in fp16 even if outputs look plausible.
when_to_show: Show after students implement softmax and observe small inaccuracies; use it as a checklist metric (sum close to 1) to compare implementations and motivate using stable formulations.
suggested_caption: Softmax sum-to-one diagnostic: Algorithm 3.1 vs alternative (Blanchard et al., 2021).

source_author: Blanchard, Pierre, Higham, Desmond J, Higham, Nicholas J
alt: Sum of entries of computed softmax vector for Algorithm 4.1 (red circles), analyzed in Theorem 4.3, and the alternative (blue crosses) analyzed in Theorem 4.4.
description: Plot of the sum of entries of computed softmax vectors for the shifted softmax algorithm (Algorithm 4.1, red circles) and its alternative version (blue crosses), across many test cases; deviations from 1 indicate loss of normalization accuracy in fp16.
teaching_value: Reinforces that shifting improves robustness but does not guarantee all variants are equally accurate; students learn to evaluate competing stable-looking algorithms using an invariant (sum equals 1).
when_to_show: Show when comparing multiple 'stable softmax' implementations; ask learners to choose an implementation based on how tightly sums cluster around 1 and relate differences to theorems/analysis.
suggested_caption: Softmax sum-to-one: shifted Algorithm 4.1 vs alternative (Blanchard et al., 2021).
